4.note: Loved by God and chosen — the election formula precisely echoes YHWH's covenant-choosing of Israel: Deuteronomy 7:6-7 ('you are a people holy to the LORD... the LORD has chosen you to be his treasured possession... because the LORD loved you'). Paul applies the Israel-election vocabulary to Gentile believers in Thessalonica without qualification, signaling that the Thessalonians are the eschatological extension of the chosen people — gathered through the gospel from among the nations.

9.note: Turned to God from idols — the conversion from idolatry to the living God echoes the prophetic polemic against idol-worship (Isaiah 44:9-20 on the absurdity of idol-making; 1 Thessalonians 45:20 'ignorant are those who carry about idols of wood'; Jeremiah 16:19-20 'our ancestors possessed nothing but false gods'). The phrase is not Paul's coinage but draws on the prophetic tradition's standard characterization of paganism versus YHWH-worship. The nations turning from idols to the living God is an OT eschatological expectation (Isaiah 2:2-4; Zechariah 8:22-23).

10.note: Jesus who rescues us from the coming wrath — the Day of the LORD's wrath is the controlling OT background (Zephaniah 1:15-16; 1 Thessalonians 2:3 'seek the LORD... seek righteousness... perhaps you will be sheltered on the day of the LORD's anger'; Isaiah 26:20 'go into your rooms and shut the doors behind you; hide yourselves for a little while until his wrath has passed by'). The rescue from wrath is not generic but eschatological: the same wrath that all the OT Day-of-the-LORD texts announced. Jesus as the one who rescues from it is the fulfillment of the prophetic promise that the righteous would be hidden from YHWH's judgment-day.

4.note: Approved by God who tests our hearts — the heart-testing language echoes Jeremiah 11:20 ('you who judge righteously and test the heart and mind') and Psalms 17:3 ('though you probe my heart... you will find that I have planned no evil'). The idea of God as the one who tests whether speech and motive are genuine — not in the sense of moral trial but of divine discernment — is rooted in the OT understanding of YHWH's omniscience (1 Samuel 16:7; Psalms 139:23). Paul claims his ministry has passed this divine scrutiny.

12.note: As a father deals with his own children — the father-son discipline and instruction metaphor echoes the OT's characterization of YHWH's relationship to Israel: Deuteronomy 8:5 ('know then in your heart that as a man disciplines his son, so the LORD disciplines you'); Hosea 11:1-4 ('when Israel was a child, I loved him... I led them with cords of human kindness'). Paul's pastoral care for the Thessalonians is an embodiment of YHWH's fatherly guidance — he represents the divine father's instruction to the covenanted child.

15.note: Who killed the Lord Jesus and the prophets — the killing-of-the-prophets accusation echoes the OT pattern of Israel's repeated rejection of YHWH's messengers: Nehemiah 9:26 ('they killed your prophets, who had warned them in order to turn them back to you'); 1 Kings 19:10 (Elijah: 'they have killed your prophets with the sword'); Jeremiah 2:30. This pattern is the lens through which Paul interprets the Thessalonians' Jewish opponents: they are repeating the covenantal failure of those who rejected YHWH's messengers throughout the prophetic era.

16.note: Filling up the measure of their sins — Genesis 15:16 ('the sin of the Amorites has not yet reached its full measure') introduces the theological principle that divine judgment waits until a nation's sin has reached its appointed measure. The same concept appears in Matthew 23:32 ('fill up the measure of the sin of your ancestors'). Paul applies this OT pattern to those who obstruct the Gentile mission: they are completing the same historical trajectory as the Amorites and the sinful predecessors who faced YHWH's judgment at the appointed fullness of time.

3.note: We are destined for these trials — the suffering-is-appointed theme echoes the OT's understanding that the righteous suffer as part of YHWH's purposes (Psalms 34:19 'the righteous person may have many troubles, but the LORD delivers them from them all'; Isaiah 48:10 'I have refined you... in the furnace of affliction'). The Greek (keimai, set/appointed) indicates divine intentionality rather than mere prediction. The Servant's suffering in Isa 53 is the paradigm: appointed suffering that produces salvific outcomes.

13.note: At the coming of our Lord Jesus with all his holy ones — Zechariah 14:5 ('then the LORD my God will come, and all the holy ones with him') is the OT source text for the parousia accompanied by the holy ones. In Zechariah's context, YHWH comes to judge the nations and establish his kingdom; Paul applies this exact coming to Christ's return. The 'holy ones' in Zechariah are likely angels (consistent with Deuteronomy 33:2-3; Psalms 89:6-7); in Paul's use they may include both angels and the previously dead believers (1 Thessalonians 4:14).

3.note: God's will is your sanctification — the holiness-command echoes Leviticus 19:2 ('be holy because I, the LORD your God, am holy'). Paul's instruction on sexual purity is not a new ethical code but the application of Israel's holiness-tradition to the messianic community. The specific context (avoiding sexual immorality, honoring the body as distinct from pagan practice) applies Levitical holiness categories — bodies set apart for YHWH — to the Thessalonian believers. The motive clause is identical: be holy because God is holy.

8.note: Who gives you his Holy Spirit — the gift of the Spirit as the content of the sanctification-command fulfills Ezekiel 36:26-27's new covenant promise: 'I will give you a new heart and put a new spirit in you... and I will put my Spirit in you and move you to follow my decrees.' Paul's warning that rejecting this instruction is rejecting God (not man) draws on Ezek 36's framing: the Spirit is given by God himself to enable covenantal obedience, and its rejection is rejection of the divine giver, not the human messenger.

9.note: Taught by God (theodidaktoi) to love one another — this rare compound (found only here in the NT) deliberately echoes Isaiah 54:13 ('all your children will be taught by the LORD') and Jeremiah 31:34 (new covenant: 'they will all know me... I will put my law in their minds and write it on their hearts'). Paul coins the term to signal that the Thessalonians' love for one another is a new-covenant phenomenon: the inner divine instruction that both Isaiah and Jeremiah promised for the eschatological age is now operative through the Spirit.

13.note: Those who sleep in death — the sleep-of-death metaphor draws on OT usage (Job 14:12; Psalms 13:3; Daniel 12:2 'many who sleep in the dust of the earth will awake'). Daniel 12:2 is particularly important as the most explicit OT resurrection text, and Paul's immediate concern (not grieving like those without hope) is directly tied to it: the Thessalonians are to understand that death-as-sleep is the OT's own promise of resurrection, so grief that presumes final loss misreads Israel's scriptures.

16.note: The dead in Christ will rise first — direct fulfillment of Daniel 12:2 ('many who sleep in the dust of the earth will awake, some to everlasting life, others to shame and everlasting contempt'). The resurrection of those who belong to Christ fulfills the specific promise of Daniel's vision. Paul's sequence — the dead rising before the living are transformed — answers the Thessalonians' concern about those who died before the Lord's return by anchoring the answer in the resurrection promise Daniel gave centuries before.

Loud command, voice of the archangel, trumpet of God — the theophany sequence (trumpet, voice from heaven, clouds) echoes the Sinai theophany (Exodus 19:16-20 'a very loud trumpet blast... the LORD descended on it in fire... Moses spoke and the voice of God answered him'; Joel 2:1 'blow the trumpet in Zion... the day of the LORD is coming'). YHWH's coming to Sinai was attended by these phenomena; the parousia of Christ is framed in the same theophanic categories, identifying it as the eschatological repetition of YHWH's paradigmatic descent.
Dead will rise -- multitude asleep in the dust will awake

17.note: Caught up in the clouds to meet the Lord in the air — Daniel 7:13 ('one like a son of man, coming with the clouds of heaven') provides the cloud-parousia framework. The clouds in Daniel are the vehicle of the divine presence (they appear at the Sinai theophany, the tabernacle, and in the divine chariot visions). Being caught up in the clouds is participation in the same divine-presence imagery: the living believers are included in the cloud-gathering that Daniel's vision depicts as the Son of Man's eschatological investiture.

2.note: The day of the Lord will come like a thief in the night — the Day-of-the-LORD formula is an OT technical term (Amos 5:18-20, where Amos warns those who desire the day that it will be darkness not light; Joel 2:1-2; Zephaniah 1:14-16 'the great day of the LORD is near — near and coming quickly'). Paul inherits this prophetic tradition and applies it to the parousia of Christ. The thief-in-the-night metaphor for the suddenness of the day is Paul's contribution (anticipating Matthew 24:43), but the underlying eschatological expectation is thoroughly OT.

3.note: Sudden destruction like labor pains on a pregnant woman — the birth-pangs metaphor for sudden, unavoidable judgment echoes Isaiah 13:8 ('terror will seize them, pain and anguish will grip them; they will writhe like a woman in labor') in the context of the Day of the LORD against Babylon. Jeremiah 4:31; Micah 4:9-10 use the same imagery for Judah's coming judgment. The metaphor communicates both inevitability (once labor pains begin they cannot be stopped) and sudden onset — exactly the quality of the unexpected Day that Paul is contrasting with the Thessalonians' prepared watchfulness.
Sudden destruction like labor pains -- day of the Lord

5.note: Children of light and children of the day — the light/darkness divide echoes Isaiah 9:2 ('the people walking in darkness have seen a great light; on those living in the land of deep darkness a light has dawned') and Isaiah 60:1-3 ('arise, shine, for your light has come'). In the OT, darkness is the metaphor for life apart from YHWH's revelation; light is the gift of the messianic age. Paul identifies the Thessalonians as belonging to the fulfilled light-age: the coming of Christ has enacted Isa 9's promised illumination, and they live as its beneficiaries.

8.note: Breastplate of faith and love, and the helmet of salvation — direct echo of Isaiah 59:17, where YHWH himself puts on the divine warrior's armor: 'He put on righteousness as his breastplate, and the helmet of salvation on his head.' Paul's armor-instruction applies to believers the equipment that in Isaiah belongs to YHWH as the divine warrior who comes to save because 'he saw that there was no one... to intervene.' The transfer of YHWH's armor to believers signals their participation in his eschatological battle; they wear what he wore when he came to rescue Israel.

23.note: May God himself, the God of peace, sanctify you through and through — the benedictory prayer echoes the Aaronic blessing pattern (Numbers 6:24-26 'the LORD bless you and keep you; the LORD make his face shine on you'). The 'God of peace' title draws on the Aaronic blessing's peace-grant; the comprehensive sanctification ('through and through... spirit, soul and body') echoes Ezekiel 36:25-27's promise of cleansing and Spirit-indwelling that constitutes the new covenant community as YHWH's holy people.

24.note: The one who calls you is faithful — the divine faithfulness as the basis for confident expectation echoes Lamentations 3:23 ('great is your faithfulness') and Deuteronomy 7:9 ('the LORD your God is God; he is the faithful God, keeping his covenant of love'). The confident assertion that God will complete what he began in the Thessalonians (sanctification, resurrection, gathering at the parousia) rests not on human perseverance but on the faithfulness of the calling God — the same attribute that sustained Israel's hope through exile and the silence of the prophets.
